"And the LORD spake unto Moses and unto Aaron, saying," The witnesses note a second muster of Levi now ordered — to take out those fit for the actual service of the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Gill notes that after the Levites were numbered and exchanged for the firstborn, they are now to be numbered a second time, to take out such as were fit for service. The witnesses note that Henry calls this a second muster of the tribe of Levi — the middle-aged men taken from among the rest to be actually employed. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — god ordering a further numbering; the the men fit for service — those of full strength taken out; the a tribe within a tribe — the able set apart from the rest; the for the tabernacle — employed in the holy work.

The puritan expositor (Henry) marks the distinction: as the whole tribe was taken out of all Israel to be God's peculiar, so the men in their strength were now taken from among the Levites to do the work of the tabernacle — a setting-apart within a setting-apart, the able committed to the heaviest and holiest service.

So the witnesses make the verse the second muster: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: God ordering a further numbering. The witnesses note the the men fit for service: those of full strength taken out. The witnesses note the a tribe within a tribe: the able set apart from the rest. The witnesses note the for the tabernacle: employed in the holy work. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: God ordering a further numbering. The expositors observe the the men fit for service: those of full strength taken out. The expositors observe the a tribe within a tribe: the able set apart from the rest. The verse thus records that God ordered a second muster of Levi — taking the men of strength from among the tribe for the actual service of the tabernacle, a setting-apart within a setting-apart.
2:

The witnesses note that Gill notes they were to begin with the Kohathites and number them first. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the Kohathites raised out of the total of the Levites, numbered first and specially, the men from thirty to fifty. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the sons of Kohath — numbered first of the Levites; the by their families — reckoned by their fathers' houses; the raised out of the sum — counted specially from the total; the the highest charge — theirs the most holy things to bear.

The witnesses agree the Kohathites were counted first because theirs was the highest charge — the bearing of the most holy things; though Kohath was Levi's second son, his house was given the place of honour, the order of the numbering following the order of the service, the most sacred trust named before the rest.

So the witnesses make the verse the Kohathites first: The witnesses note the the sons of Kohath: numbered first of the Levites. The witnesses note the by their families: reckoned by their fathers' houses. The witnesses note the raised out of the sum: counted specially from the total. 3:
"From thirty years old and upward even until fifty years old, all that enter into the host, to do the work in the tabernacle of the congregation." The witnesses note the age of Levite service — from thirty to fifty, the prime of a man's strength. The witnesses note that Gill notes thirty is the full strength of man and fifty the beginning of decline, the Levites setting up and taking down the tabernacle and bearing its burdens on their shoulders. The witnesses note that he marks how both John the Baptist and Christ himself entered on their public work at about thirty years. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from thirty — the full strength of a man; the until fifty — when strength begins to decline; the the heavy work — setting up, taking down, and bearing the tabernacle; the Christ at thirty — the Lord beginning his ministry at that age (Luke 3:23).

The witnesses agree the appointed span fitted the labour to the strength: the heavy work of carrying the tabernacle demanded men in their full vigour, neither too young nor too old; Gill finds it fitting that the forerunner and the Lord himself began their ministry at thirty (Luke 3:23) — the age of ripened strength for the service of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the age of service: The witnesses note the from thirty: the full strength of a man. The witnesses note the until fifty: when strength begins to decline. The witnesses note the the heavy work: setting up, taking down, and bearing the tabernacle. The witnesses note the Christ at thirty: the Lord beginning his ministry at that age (Luke 3:23). The expositors observe the from thirty: the full strength of a man. The expositors observe the until fifty: when strength begins to decline. The verse thus records that the Levites served from thirty to fifty, the prime of strength — the very age, Gill notes, at which the Lord himself began his ministry.
4:
"This shall be the service of the sons of Kohath in the tabernacle of the congregation, about the most holy things:" The witnesses note the Kohathites' charge — the most holy things — and the wisdom of assigning each his office. The witnesses note that Gill notes the service of Kohath concerned the most holy things, set out in what follows. The witnesses note that Calvin sees God assigning the Levites their various offices, lest their promiscuous zeal beget confusion, and lest ambition stir some to push themselves forward. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the most holy things — the highest and most sacred charge; the various offices assigned — each Levite given his own work; the lest confusion — that promiscuous zeal beget no disorder; the lest ambition — that none push himself forward.

The reformed reader (Calvin) draws the lesson of order in God's house: men work confusedly unless a rule is prescribed, so God parcelled out the offices, that none run about in aimless hurry nor intrude on another's charge — a wisdom for the church, where each is to keep his own place and serve in his own gift, that contention be avoided and the work be done.

So the witnesses make the verse the Kohathites' office: The witnesses note the the most holy things: the highest and most sacred charge. The witnesses note the various offices assigned: each Levite given his own work. The witnesses note the lest confusion: that promiscuous zeal beget no disorder. The witnesses note the lest ambition: that none push himself forward. The expositors observe the the most holy things: the highest and most sacred charge. The expositors observe the various offices assigned: each Levite given his own work. The expositors observe the lest confusion: that promiscuous zeal beget no disorder. The expositors observe the lest ambition: that none push himself forward. The believer is taught to mark the the most holy things, the various offices assigned, the lest confusion, the lest ambition, and to reverence the holy things of God. The verse thus records that the Kohathites were charged with the most holy things — God assigning each his office, as Calvin marks, that confusion and ambition be avoided in the service of his house.
5:
"And when the camp setteth forward, Aaron shall come, and his sons, and they shall take down the covering vail, and cover the ark of testimony with it:" The witnesses note that when the camp moved, Aaron and his sons — not the Levites — first covered the ark with the veil. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was upon the cloud's removing and the trumpets blowing. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set vv.5-15 together — the priests covering each holy thing before the Kohathites might bear it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the camp setteth forward — the signal to break up and march; the Aaron and his sons — the priests alone handling the ark; the the covering veil — the ark hidden under the veil; the the holiest screened — the most sacred object covered first.

The witnesses agree the priests alone handled and wrapped the sacred furniture: the ark of the testimony was first hidden under the veil by Aaron and his sons, that the holiest object be screened from every common eye and hand before the march; the covering of the ark with the very veil that had hung before it is a striking figure of the hiding of the holiest until the appointed time.

So the witnesses make the verse the covering of the ark: The witnesses note the the camp setteth forward: the signal to break up and march. The witnesses note the Aaron and his sons: the priests alone handling the ark. The witnesses note the the covering veil: the ark hidden under the veil. The witnesses note the the holiest screened: the most sacred object covered first. The expositors observe the the camp setteth forward: the signal to break up and march. The verse thus records that the priests covered the ark with the veil before the march — the holiest screened from every common eye, a figure of the hiding of the most sacred until its time.
6:
"And shall put thereon the covering of badgers' skins, and shall spread over it a cloth wholly of blue, and shall put in the staves thereof." The witnesses note the threefold covering of the ark — the veil, the badgers' skins, and a cloth wholly of blue — and the staves set in. The witnesses note that Gill notes the badgers' skins were a covering made on purpose for the march, to preserve the ark from rain and dust, laid over the other coverings. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the staves, removed while the ark was covered, were then put back in their places (Exod. 25:15). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the badgers' skins — a covering against rain and dust; the a cloth wholly of blue — the heavenly colour outermost; the the staves put in — set back for the bearing; the three coverings — the ark most carefully wrapped of all.

The witnesses agree the ark was the most carefully wrapped of all, having three coverings where lesser things had fewer: the cloth wholly of blue, the colour of heaven, lay outermost in sight, that the holiest thing might be borne through the wilderness clothed in a heavenly hue — the ark of God's presence shrouded yet honoured, hidden from gaze but carried with reverence.

So the witnesses make the verse the wrapped ark: The witnesses note the the badgers' skins: a covering against rain and dust. The witnesses note the a cloth wholly of blue: the heavenly colour outermost. The witnesses note the the staves put in: set back for the bearing. The witnesses note the three coverings: the ark most carefully wrapped of all. The expositors observe the the badgers' skins: a covering against rain and dust. 7:
"And upon the table of shewbread they shall spread a cloth of blue, and put thereon the dishes, and the spoons, and the bowls, and covers to cover withal: and the continual bread shall be thereon." The witnesses note the covering of the shewbread table, the bread continuing upon it even through the marches. The witnesses note that Gill notes the cloth of blue was spread that the table also might not be seen nor touched by the Levites. The witnesses note that Ellicott observes the shewbread is called "continual" because it was renewed every sabbath and was continually before the LORD, even during the marches through the desert (Lev. 24:5). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a cloth of blue — the table screened from sight and touch; the the dishes and bowls — the vessels set upon it; the the continual bread — remaining even through the marches; the unbroken fellowship — the table never empty before the LORD.

The witnesses agree the bread of the Presence was never withdrawn: it remained on the table even as Israel journeyed, wrapped with it under the blue cloth, that the symbol of the twelve tribes set before God might be continual indeed — the fellowship of God and his people unbroken on the march as in the camp, the table never empty before him.

So the witnesses make the verse the covered table: The witnesses note the a cloth of blue: the table screened from sight and touch. The witnesses note the the dishes and bowls: the vessels set upon it. The witnesses note the the continual bread: remaining even through the marches. 8:

The witnesses agree the colours of the coverings were not without meaning to the eye of faith: the table bore blue and scarlet beneath the badgers' skins, the holy things borne through the desert clothed in the hues of heaven and of sacrifice; each piece was wrapped in order and carried by its staves, the whole sanctuary made portable yet kept sacred, every covering guarding what lay beneath from common touch.

So the witnesses make the verse the table's coverings: The witnesses note the a cloth of scarlet: spread over the vessels and bread. 9:

The witnesses note that Gill notes the candlestick had lamps that were continually lighted and gave light in the holy place. The witnesses note that all the instruments of the candlestick — tongs, snuffdishes, oil vessels — were covered with it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a cloth of blue — the candlestick wrapped for the march; the the lamps — the light of the holy place; the the tongs and vessels — all its instruments covered with it; the the light borne onward — carried concealed yet never extinguished.

The witnesses agree the lampstand, the giver of light in the sanctuary, was itself wrapped and hidden for the journey: the light that burned continually before the LORD was carried covered through the dark wilderness, its vessels gathered with it; the church's light, though hidden for a season under the old order, was borne onward toward the land, never extinguished though for a time concealed.

So the witnesses make the verse the covered candlestick: The witnesses note the a cloth of blue: the candlestick wrapped for the march. The witnesses note the the lamps: the light of the holy place. 10:
"And they shall put it and all the vessels thereof within a covering of badgers' skins, and shall put it upon a bar." The witnesses note the candlestick wrapped in badgers' skins and laid upon a bar to be borne. The witnesses note that Gill notes the badgers' skins were here the outermost covering, and that the candlestick had only two coverings, being less sacred than the ark and table, which had three. The witnesses note that it was set upon a bar for carrying. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a covering of badgers' skins — the outermost wrapping; the upon a bar — laid on a bar to be borne; the only two coverings — fewer than the ark and table; the ranks of sanctity — each wrapped according to its holiness.

The witnesses agree the number of coverings marked the degrees of holiness: the ark and the shewbread table had three coverings, but the candlestick only two, a fine gradation showing that even among the holy things there were ranks of sanctity, the most sacred most carefully hidden; nothing was left uncovered, yet each was wrapped according to its place, the order of holiness kept in the very manner of carrying.

So the witnesses make the verse the gradation of holiness: The witnesses note the a covering of badgers' skins: the outermost wrapping. The witnesses note the upon a bar: laid on a bar to be borne. The witnesses note the only two coverings: fewer than the ark and table. The witnesses note the ranks of sanctity: each wrapped according to its holiness. 11:

The witnesses note the covering of the golden altar of incense with blue and badgers' skins, its staves set in. The witnesses note that Gill notes the altar of incense was overlaid with gold, and so called the golden altar. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads "put in the staves," as with the ark and table. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the golden altar — the altar of incense overlaid with gold; the a cloth of blue — wrapped for the march; the the badgers' skins — the outer covering; the the staves — set in for the bearing.

The witnesses agree the altar of incense, the place of the daily fragrant offering, was likewise wrapped and borne: the golden altar from which prayer ascended before God was carried covered through the wilderness, that the ministry of intercession, figured by the incense, might go with Israel on every march; the holy things of worship were not left behind but borne onward, the whole apparatus of God's service moving with his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the covered altar of incense: The witnesses note the the golden altar: the altar of incense overlaid with gold. The witnesses note the a cloth of blue: wrapped for the march. The witnesses note the the badgers' skins: the outer covering. The witnesses note the the staves: set in for the bearing. 12:

The witnesses agree nothing of the sanctuary's service was left uncared for: even the smaller vessels of ministry were gathered, wrapped, and borne with the same reverence as the greater, that the whole furniture of worship be kept holy and entire; the care reaching to the least instrument teaches that in God's service no part is too small for reverence, every vessel of the sanctuary precious in its place.

13:

The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the altar of burnt offering in the court, on which sacrifices were daily burnt, so that ashes were always to be removed, the more needful when the tabernacle was taken down; and the fire, though not mentioned, was doubtless preserved. The witnesses note that the purple cloth was then spread over the cleansed altar. 14:

15:
"And when Aaron and his sons have made an end of covering the sanctuary, and all the vessels of the sanctuary... the sons of Kohath shall come to bear it: but they shall not touch any holy thing, lest they die." The witnesses agree the death-penalty fenced the holiest from presumptuous touch: the Levites carried the sacred furniture, but the priests alone might handle it uncovered, and to lay a hand on the holy thing itself was to die — a strictness later proved in Uzzah (2 Sam. 6:6-7); the lesson stands that nearness to holy things is no warrant for familiarity, and that God will be sanctified in them that come near him.

16:
"And to the office of Eleazar the son of Aaron the priest pertaineth the oil for the light, and the sweet incense, and the daily meat offering, and the anointing oil, and the oversight of all the tabernacle..." The witnesses note the charge of Eleazar — the oil, the incense, the daily offering, the anointing oil, and the oversight of all the tabernacle.
